problem_summary: 2015 MCM Problem C (interdisciplinary): the "ICM" simulation company asks teams to build a Human Capital Network model of a single organization (370 positions across a given org chart, 7 job types), simulate organizational churn at an 18% annual turnover rate over a decade, quantify the effect on productivity, forecast 2-year recruiting and training budget (in units of σ), and stress-test the model under higher/forced turnover (Tasks 4 and 5). This team (control #33572) modeled the firm as a 4-layer multiplex network driven by a month-by-month event automaton, computed a composite "demission index" to rank who quits, and ran Monte-Carlo-style simulations to answer all five tasks.
models_used: Multiplex (multilayer) network as the core organizational model: 4 superimposed graphs over the same node set X (employees) — Department Network G_H, Friendship Network G_F, Information-Flow Network G_M, Leadership Network G_L — with inter-layer coupling edges C linking the same person across layers (def. M = (G, C))

Department Network: directed tree built from the occupation-distribution diagram (parent dept -> child dept edges encode promotion paths); department-influence index I_H = H_D/(H_M - 1), driven by vacancy ratio within a department
Friendship Network: undirected graph; influence via Degree Centrality, friendship index I_F = 1/(F_S + 1) to model herd-mentality contagion of discontent
Information-Flow Network: undirected communication graph scored with the PageRank algorithm, then squashed: message index I_M = tansig(PR^(x)) (hyperbolic-tangent activation)
Leadership Network: directed tree (boss -> subordinate); leader-changing index I_L = max over leaving leaders of 1/distance(x, y) (shortest-path distance to a departed superior)
Occupation-dissatisfaction index I_P: piecewise-linear saturation of ability score between a position threshold Ab_N and a growth cap Ab_G (0 below threshold, linear ramp above)

Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) to derive the 5-index weight vector for the composite demission index (weights: W_O=0.5992 dissatisfaction, W_F=0.1926 friendship, W_H=0.0795 department, W_L=0.0795 leadership, W_M=0.0492 message)
Demission index as a weighted linear combination I_D = W·I of the five effect indexes, used to rank employees by quit-probability
Finite-automaton / discrete-event time-stepped simulation: monthly update cycle of 5 ordered events — Demission -> Promotion -> Transfer -> Recruitment (start of month) -> Settlement (end of month)
Poisson process for stochastic arrivals/departures: per-position monthly resign and recruit counts drawn from a Poisson random function R_P (e.g. PL^(p) = R_P(NP^(p)·P^(p)/12))
Two-tier churn-rate decomposition: middle managers (JM/ES/IS) churn at P_M = 2·NP_S·NP_M·P_C/((NP_S+NP_M)·NP_M), other roles at P_O = P_M/2, encoding the problem's '2x manager turnover' stipulation
Ability-growth threshold system: per-level promotion thresholds and monthly ability accrual (Table 2), with growth cap Ab_B = 1.3·Ab_N, governing promotion eligibility

Steady-state analytical check: closed-form equilibrium headcount N_S = (370·2/3)/(P_C + 2/3) from balancing expected departures against expected hires, used to validate the simulation (predicts 291 at 18%, matching simulated band)
Productivity model: per-employee Pro = Ab - weak·Ab with weak = 0.5·I_D; organization productivity = sum over employees
key_techniques: Mapping a vague HR problem onto established network-science primitives, then borrowing a named, citable algorithm for each layer (Degree Centrality for friendship, PageRank for information flow, shortest-path distance for leadership, vacancy ratio for department) — each index has a one-line real-world justification (herd mentality, bad-mood contagion, morale collapse when a boss leaves)
Separating a STATIC structure (the multiplex network + indexes) from a DYNAMIC driver (the 5-event monthly automaton), so the same scored network is repeatedly mutated by well-defined events
Using AHP to convert a fuzzy 'which factor matters most' judgment into a defensible numeric weight vector, making the otherwise-arbitrary demission-index coefficients look principled

Embedding the problem's hard constraints directly into the math: the '2x manager churn' rule becomes the P_M/P_O split; '370 positions' and '2/3 hire fraction' become the steady-state formula; 'months' becomes the simulation timestep

Poisson-driven Monte Carlo: running the simulation multiple times (DATA1/DATA2/DATA3 in the budget table; three simulation curves in Figs 9-10) to show the budget answer is robust to randomness and to distinguish stochastic (recruiting) from deterministic (training) costs
Independent analytical cross-check of a simulation output (steady-state headcount formula vs. simulated band) — a strong validity argument judges reward

A dynamic occupation-distribution rule (priority-ordered, top-to-bottom/left-to-right, one-slot-at-a-time, with swap-down backfilling) that makes the org chart self-healing and the model reusable across org structures

Reusing the same five-index dashboard (I_O, I_H, I_F, I_M, I_L) as the unit of analysis in every task (churn, sensitivity, extreme scenario), giving the whole paper a consistent explanatory vocabulary
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins because it makes a non-quantitative 'company simulation' prompt fully quantitative with a coherent, original mechanism: a multiplex network is exactly the right abstraction for an employee who simultaneously has a boss, friends, a department, and a communication footprint, and the team executes that abstraction end-to-end. Every modeling choice is anchored to a citable method (PageRank, Degree Centrality, AHP, automata theory) rather than invented ad hoc, which signals literacy and defensibility. Critically, it answers ALL five tasks with one unified model instead of bolting on disconnected sub-models, and it backs the simulation with an independent closed-form steady-state check (predicted 291 vs simulated band at 18%) — judges reward a model that validates itself. The numbers are concrete and reproduced under randomness (recruiting 32.8σ, training 141.79σ; three Monte-Carlo replicates), the sensitivity analysis gives a crisp, decision-relevant verdict ('ICM cannot sustain 80% occupancy at 25% or 35% churn'), and the extreme-scenario task shows the model degrading gracefully (56.8% occupancy) rather than breaking — demonstrating robustness, which is explicitly part of the prompt.
section_highlights: The structure mirrors the task list and reads top-down: Background -> Problem Restatement/Analysis -> Bold Assumptions -> Symbol table -> Model Preparation (occupation distribution) -> the 4-layer network build -> the 5-event dynamic process -> one numbered section per task (Two through Five) -> Strengths/Weaknesses -> References. Strong moves: (1) an explicit, labeled 'Bold Assumptions' block and a separate distribution-assumptions block, so every simplification is visible and owned; (2) heavy use of figures as the narrative spine — Fig 3 shows the same 7-person division rendered three ways (friendship/info/leadership), Fig 4 stacks them into the multiplex, Fig 5 is the event-order flowchart, Figs 6/8/11/12 are the result time-series — captions do real explanatory work, not just labels; (3) algorithms written as numbered Step One/Two/... pseudocode for each event (promotion, transfer with 6 steps + substeps, recruitment with swap logic), making the simulation auditable; (4) consistent symbol notation and a reused five-index dashboard tied together by the AHP-weighted demission equation that is restated wherever it is used; (5) an honest Strengths/Weaknesses section that names the real soft spots (random intra-layer edges, omitted subjective/family factors).
pitfalls_or_limitations: The three non-Department layers (friendship, information flow, leadership intra-edges) are populated with synthetic/random edges because no real data exists — the team admits this; it means index values like I_F, I_M, I_L rest on assumed graph topology, so the demission ranking is only as good as those guesses

AHP weights are derived from the team's own pairwise judgments with no reported consistency ratio and no calibration to data — the headline coefficient W_O=0.5992 effectively predetermines that dissatisfaction dominates churn, a near-circular design choice

Several formulas are stated without derivation or units: Ab_B = 1.3·Ab_N (and special 1.5x cases), weak = 0.5·I_D (the 0.5 is unjustified), and tansig(PR) squashing PageRank into [0,1) is asserted rather than motivated

The data tables show OCR/transcription artifacts and physically implausible monotone ramps (e.g., Fig-9/Fig-10 stacked-bar columns that increase by a fixed 0.5 each row, training cost pinned at a flat 0.5/6.0) suggesting placeholder or corrupted figure data rather than genuine simulation output in the markdown version

Productivity is defined as Pro = Ab - 0.5·I_D·Ab, i.e. the five 'soft' indexes only ever subtract from output and never add — morale/teamwork can only hurt productivity in this model, which is a one-sided and arguably unrealistic assumption

Internal inconsistencies in reported figures: text says the four indexes but five effect indexes are used; the sensitivity section quotes an 'expected number of staffs 296' that does not match the per-rate ideal values (291/269/242); summary cites 56.8% occupancy under extreme conditions while Task 5 figure shows headcount falling to ~200-210 of 370 (~57%) — numbers are loosely reconciled
Heavy reliance on English-as-second-language prose with grammatical roughness throughout; the modeling carries the paper, not the writing polish
Model is fit to ICM's specific 370-position org chart; generality is claimed (via the dynamic distribution rule) but never demonstrated on a second structure
reusable_patterns: Multiplex-network template for any 'one entity, many simultaneous relationships' problem (employees, cities, users): one node set, several typed layers, an inter-layer coupling, and one scalar index per layer aggregated into a single decision score
Static-structure + discrete-event-automaton split: score a network, then evolve it with a fixed, ordered list of monthly/daily events — clean, debuggable, and easy to explain with a flowchart

Per-layer 'borrow a named algorithm + justify with a real-world story' pattern: pick PageRank/centrality/shortest-path for each relationship type and attach a one-sentence mechanism (contagion, morale, herd behavior) — instantly defensible and citable
AHP to manufacture defensible weights when you must combine heterogeneous indices and have no training data (state the weight vector explicitly so it's auditable)

Always pair a simulation answer with an independent closed-form sanity check (here, the steady-state headcount formula N_S = (Q·2/3)/(P_C+2/3)) — derive an equilibrium analytically and show the simulation lands in that band

Encode the problem's hard constraints directly as model parameters (the 2x-manager-churn rule -> P_M/P_O split; the recruit fraction -> equilibrium formula) so graders can see you respected the prompt literally
Run multiple Poisson/Monte-Carlo replicates and report them side by side (DATA1/2/3) to demonstrate robustness and to separate stochastic outputs from deterministic ones
Reuse a single small multi-index dashboard across every task so sensitivity and extreme-scenario sections speak the same quantitative language as the base case
Self-healing assignment rule (priority list + swap-down backfill) to keep a structured population valid as agents enter/leave — reusable for any staffing/assignment ABM
Write each simulation step as numbered Step One/Two pseudocode in the paper so the dynamic model is transparent and reproducible to judges
